questions.q: Of how many Houses does the Parliament of India consist?
questions.explanation: Parliament is bicameral, consisting of the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha.

questions.q: Which two Houses make up the Parliament of India?
questions.explanation: Parliament comprises the Lok Sabha (House of the People) and the Rajya Sabha (Council of States).

questions.q: Besides the two Houses, who else is a component of the Parliament of India?
questions.explanation: Under the Constitution, Parliament consists of the President and the two Houses.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the Parliament of India?
questions.explanation: Parliament is the supreme legislative body that makes laws for the country.

questions.q: The Lok Sabha is also known as the:
questions.explanation: The Lok Sabha is the lower house, called the House of the People.

questions.q: The Rajya Sabha is also known as the:
questions.explanation: The Rajya Sabha is the upper house, called the Council of States.

questions.q: A proposed law placed before Parliament is called a:
questions.explanation: A bill is a draft legislative proposal that becomes an Act when passed and assented to.

questions.q: After both Houses pass a bill, whose assent is required for it to become law?
questions.explanation: A bill becomes an Act only after it receives the assent of the President.

questions.q: In which city is the Parliament of India located?
questions.explanation: The Parliament of India is situated in New Delhi, the national capital.

questions.q: Apart from making laws, Parliament also exercises oversight over which branch?
questions.explanation: Parliament oversees the executive, to which the Council of Ministers is responsible.

questions.q: The provisions relating to the Parliament of India are found mainly in which Part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Part V of the Constitution deals with the Union, including Parliament, in Articles 79 to 122.

questions.q: Which Article of the Constitution provides that there shall be a Parliament for the Union?
questions.explanation: Article 79 states that there shall be a Parliament consisting of the President and the two Houses.

questions.q: A bill dealing solely with taxation and government spending is classified as a:
questions.explanation: A money bill concerns matters such as taxation, borrowing, and expenditure from the Consolidated Fund.

questions.q: A money bill can be introduced only in which House?
questions.explanation: Under Article 110, a money bill can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha.

questions.q: Who has the power to summon and prorogue the Houses of Parliament?
questions.explanation: Under Article 85, the President summons, prorogues, and may dissolve the House of the People.

questions.q: The maximum gap permitted between two sessions of Parliament is:
questions.explanation: Article 85 requires that no more than six months elapse between the last sitting of one session and the first of the next.

questions.q: Who presides over a joint sitting of the two Houses of Parliament?
options: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: Under Article 118, the Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting of both Houses.

questions.q: A joint sitting of both Houses may be summoned to resolve a deadlock on which type of bill?
questions.explanation: Article 108 allows a joint sitting to break a deadlock on an ordinary bill; it does not apply to money or constitution amendment bills.

questions.q: The presiding officer who provides for joint sittings is dealt with under which provision?
questions.explanation: Article 108 provides for joint sittings of both Houses in cases of disagreement over a bill.

questions.q: The first sitting of independent India's Parliament after the first general election took place in:
questions.explanation: The first Lok Sabha was constituted after the 1951-52 general election, with its first session in 1952.

questions.q: Which official maintains a record of the proceedings and is the administrative head of a House's secretariat?
questions.explanation: Each House has a Secretary-General who heads its secretariat and oversees its records and administration.

questions.q: The power of the President to promulgate ordinances when Parliament is not in session is granted by which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 123 empowers the President to promulgate ordinances when both Houses are not in session.

questions.q: An ordinance promulgated by the President must be laid before Parliament and ceases to operate unless approved within:
questions.explanation: Under Article 123, an ordinance ceases to operate six weeks after Parliament reassembles unless approved earlier.

questions.q: The privileges of the Houses of Parliament and their members are dealt with under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 105 deals with the powers, privileges, and immunities of the Houses of Parliament and their members.

questions.q: The quorum required to constitute a sitting of either House is:
questions.explanation: Article 100 fixes the quorum at one-tenth of the total number of members of the House.

questions.q: The annual financial statement, or budget, is laid before Parliament under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 112 requires the annual financial statement of the Union to be laid before both Houses.

questions.q: If a member of either House is absent without permission for how long may the seat be declared vacant?
questions.explanation: Article 101 allows a seat to be declared vacant if a member is absent for sixty days without permission of the House.

questions.q: A new Parliament building in New Delhi was inaugurated in which year?
questions.explanation: A new Parliament building was inaugurated in 2023, replacing the earlier Lutyens-Baker structure for legislative functions.

questions.q: The rules regulating the procedure and conduct of business in each House are framed under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 118 empowers each House to make rules for regulating its procedure and conduct of business.

questions.q: Article 122 bars courts from inquiring into proceedings of Parliament on the ground of:
questions.explanation: Article 122 provides that the validity of parliamentary proceedings shall not be questioned on the ground of any alleged irregularity of procedure.
